name: Matrices
subject: Maths

sections.heading: 3.1 Matrix — Definition & Order
sections.body:
A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers (or functions) arranged in rows and columns. A matrix with $m$ rows and $n$ columns is said to have order $m\times n$. We write $A=[a_{ij}]_{m\times n}$ where $a_{ij}$ is the entry in row $i$, column $j$.

Two matrices are equal if they have the same order and corresponding entries are equal.

sections.heading: 3.2 Types of Matrices
sections.body:
• Row matrix: $1\times n$. Column matrix: $m\times 1$.
  • Square matrix: $m=n$. Diagonal entries: $a_{ii}$.
  • Diagonal matrix: square with $a_{ij}=0$ for $i\neq j$.
  • Scalar matrix: diagonal with all diagonal entries equal.
  • Identity matrix $I_n$: scalar with diagonal entries $=1$.
  • Zero matrix $O$: every entry $0$.
  • Symmetric: $A^{T}=A$. Skew-symmetric: $A^{T}=-A$ (so diagonal entries $=0$).

sections.heading: 3.3 Addition, Scalar Multiplication, Subtraction
sections.body:
For matrices of the same order:

  • $(A+B)_{ij}=a_{ij}+b_{ij}$.
  • $(kA)_{ij}=k\,a_{ij}$ for scalar $k$.
  • $A-B=A+(-1)B$.

Properties: $A+B=B+A$, $(A+B)+C=A+(B+C)$, $A+O=A$, $A+(-A)=O$. Distributivity over addition: $k(A+B)=kA+kB$.

sections.heading: 3.4 Matrix Multiplication
sections.body:
Product $AB$ is defined only when the number of columns of $A$ equals the number of rows of $B$. If $A$ is $m\times p$ and $B$ is $p\times n$, then $AB$ is $m\times n$ with

$(AB)_{ij}=\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{ik}\,b_{kj}.$

Properties:

  • Associative: $(AB)C=A(BC)$.
  • Distributive: $A(B+C)=AB+AC$.
  • Not commutative in general: $AB\neq BA$.
  • Existence of identity: $AI=IA=A$.
  • $AB=O$ does NOT imply $A=O$ or $B=O$.

sections.heading: 3.5 Transpose & Symmetric Decomposition
sections.body:
$A^{T}$ is obtained by interchanging rows and columns: $(A^{T})_{ij}=a_{ji}$.

Properties:

  • $(A^{T})^{T}=A$.
  • $(A+B)^{T}=A^{T}+B^{T}$.
  • $(kA)^{T}=k\,A^{T}$.
  • $(AB)^{T}=B^{T}A^{T}$ (reverse-order law).

Symmetric/Skew decomposition. Every square matrix can be expressed uniquely as $A=P+Q$ where $P=\dfrac{1}{2}(A+A^{T})$ is symmetric and $Q=\dfrac{1}{2}(A-A^{T})$ is skew-symmetric.

sections.heading: 3.6 Elementary Row/Column Operations
sections.body:
Three elementary operations on a matrix:

  1. Interchange two rows: $R_i\leftrightarrow R_j$.
  2. Multiply a row by a non-zero scalar: $R_i\to k\,R_i$.
  3. Add a multiple of one row to another: $R_i\to R_i+k\,R_j$.

Same for columns. Used to reduce matrices to row-echelon form and to compute inverses by Gauss-Jordan.

sections.heading: 3.7 Invertible Matrix (by Elementary Operations)
sections.body:
A square matrix $A$ is invertible if there exists $B$ with $AB=BA=I$. Then $B=A^{-1}$ and is unique.

Method. Write $A=IA$. Apply a sequence of row operations to the LHS until it becomes $I$; performing the SAME operations on the RHS converts $I$ into $A^{-1}$. Hence we obtain $I=A^{-1}A$.

Properties: $(A^{-1})^{-1}=A$, $(AB)^{-1}=B^{-1}A^{-1}$, $(A^{T})^{-1}=(A^{-1})^{T}$.

summary: A matrix is an array $[a_{ij}]_{m\times n}$. Add/subtract entrywise (same order); multiply with the row-by-column rule which forces cols$(A)=$rows$(B)$. Multiplication is associative and distributive but not commutative. Transpose obeys $(AB)^{T}=B^{T}A^{T}$. Every square matrix splits uniquely into symmetric $+$ skew-symmetric parts via $\tfrac12(A\pm A^{T})$. Elementary row operations let you find $A^{-1}$ from $A=IA$.
pitfalls: Multiplying matrices of incompatible orders — check cols$(A)=$rows$(B)$.
Cancelling: $AB=AC$ does NOT give $B=C$ unless $A$ is invertible.
Mixing row and column operations during a single inversion run — pick one mode.
